summary: French Pointillist/Neo-Impressionist painter. Best known for Portrait of Félix Fénéon (art critic holding a flower in front of swirling colorful background). Developed pointillism with Georges Seurat. Wrote From Eugène Delacroix to Neo-Impressionism. Painted In the Time of Harmony and scenes at Saint-Tropez.

works.name: Portrait of Félix Fénéon (1890)
works.description: Signac's best-known work. Depicts the anarchist art critic Félix Fénéon holding a top hat and a white cyclamen flower in front of a swirling, colorful background of green-and-red ripples and yellow stars on blue, demonstrating his Divisionist style.

clues.clue: Portrait of anarchist art critic Félix Fénéon holding a top hat and a white flower/cyclamen in front of a swirling, colorful background
examples: painted art critic Felix Feneon in front of a swirling background of different colors and patterns
depicting a man holding a top hat and a white flower, showing his Divisionist style
depicts art critic Felix Feneon, holding a flower and top hat before heavy-swirling colors

clues.clue: Background has green-and-red ripples and yellow stars on blue in swirling sections
examples: Green-and-red ripples and yellow stars on blue make up two of the swirling background sections

works.name: In the Time of Harmony (1893–1895)
works.description: Large painting subtitled 'the Golden Age is not in the Past, it is in the Future.' Depicts people relaxing by the sea: a man picks a fig from a tree, a woman feeds a fig to her child, two men play boules, a rooster, a hen, dancers under a tree, a painter with an easel, and bowlers.

clues.clue: Subtitled 'the Golden Age is not in the Past, it is in the Future'; depicts people relaxing by the sea, including men playing boules, a man picking a fig, a woman feeding her child
examples: a rooster, a hen, dancers under a tree, a painter with an easel, and bowlers appear in a work by this man showing people relaxing by the sea
subtitled 'the Golden Age is not in the Past, it is in the Future.' This painting's foreground depicts a man picking a fig from a tree

clues.clue: Henri Edmond Cross contrasted its 'dreamed-of age of happiness' with the 'poignant misery' of other anarchist paintings
examples: Henri Edmond Cross contrasted the 'dreamed-of age of happiness and well-being' depicted in this painting with the 'poignant misery' of other anarchist paintings

works.name: Sunday
works.description: A painting showing a brown-and-white cat on a Persian rug as a woman in violet stands at the window while her husband tends an off-panel fire.

clues.clue: A brown-and-white cat on a Persian rug; a woman in violet at the window; her husband tends an off-panel fire
examples: A brown-and-white cat stands on a Persian rug in a painting by this artist in which a woman in violet stands at the window while her husband sits in a chair and tends an off-panel fire

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: French Pointillist/Divisionist/Neo-Impressionist. Developed pointillism with Georges Seurat. Wrote From Eugène Delacroix to Neo-Impressionism. Criticized 'muddy mixtures.' Bought Matisse's Luxe, Calme et Volupté, inspiring Fauvism. Co-founded the Salon des Indépendants. Frequently depicted Saint-Tropez. Was a juror for the Prix Blumenthal.

comprehensive_summary:
Paul Signac was a French Neo-Impressionist painter who developed pointillism alongside Georges Seurat. His best-known work is the Portrait of Félix Fénéon (1890), depicting the anarchist art critic holding a top hat and a white cyclamen flower before a swirling, colorful background of green-and-red ripples and yellow stars on blue, demonstrating his Divisionist style. His major painting In the Time of Harmony (subtitled 'the Golden Age is not in the Past, it is in the Future') shows an idyllic scene of people relaxing by the sea, including men playing boules, a man picking a fig from a tree, a woman feeding her child, dancers under a tree, and a painter with an easel. Henri Edmond Cross contrasted its 'dreamed-of age of happiness' with the 'poignant misery' of other anarchist works. Another painting, Sunday, depicts a brown-and-white cat on a Persian rug as a woman in violet stands at the window.

Signac wrote From Eugène Delacroix to Neo-Impressionism, in which he criticized 'muddy mixtures' and echoed a Romantic painter's claim that 'the enemy of all painting is grey.' He purchased Matisse's Luxe, Calme et Volupté, inspiring Matisse toward 'color structure' and the beginning of Fauvism. He co-founded the Salon des Indépendants with Seurat and Redon, served as a juror for the Prix Blumenthal, and frequently depicted scenes at Saint-Tropez. His works were deliberately barred from the 1889 Volpini Exhibition, which instead debuted Gauguin's Post-Impressionist works.
